agent_output: {"executive_summary":"A food delivery platform connecting restaurants, customers, and delivery drivers — designed as a modular system with six bounded contexts. The architecture uses event-driven communication between contexts to keep coupling low, a central Order context as the orchestrating core domain, and clear separation between the real-time operational path (ordering, dispatch, tracking) and the analytical/back-office path. The design prioritizes independent deployability of each context, eventual consistency where safe, and strong consistency within order and payment boundaries.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Customer Gateway","responsibility":"Customer-facing API: browsing restaurants/menus, managing cart, placing orders, tracking delivery, rating. BFF pattern — tailored for mobile and web clients.","boundaries":"Owns no domain state. Aggregates data from Restaurant Catalog, Order Management, and Delivery contexts. Stateless."},{"name":"Restaurant Gateway","responsibility":"Restaurant-facing API: menu management, order acceptance/rejection, preparation status updates, payout history.","boundaries":"Owns no domain state. Delegates to Restaurant Management, Order Management, and Payment contexts."},{"name":"Driver Gateway","responsibility":"Driver-facing API: availability toggling, delivery offers, navigation, earnings dashboard.","boundaries":"Stateless BFF for driver apps. Delegates to Delivery and Payment contexts."},{"name":"Restaurant Catalog Service","responsibility":"Menu CRUD, restaurant profiles, hours, availability zones, search and discovery. Maintains a read-optimized search index.","boundaries":"Owns restaurant and menu data. Publishes MenuUpdated, RestaurantAvailabilityChanged events."},{"name":"Order Management Service","responsibility":"Core orchestrator. Manages the full order lifecycle state machine: Created → Confirmed → Preparing → ReadyForPickup → PickedUp → Delivered → Completed (with cancel/refund branches). Coordinates across restaurant confirmation, payment capture, and driver dispatch.","boundaries":"Owns Order aggregate. Publishes OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, OrderCancelled, OrderCompleted events. Uses a saga/process manager for the multi-step order flow."},{"name":"Delivery Service","responsibility":"Driver pool management, real-time location tracking, dispatch algorithm (matching orders to nearby available drivers), route optimization, ETA calculation.","boundaries":"Owns Driver aggregate (availability, location, active deliveries). Publishes DriverAssigned, DeliveryPickedUp, DeliveryCompleted events. Consumes OrderConfirmed to trigger dispatch."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment authorization on order placement, capture on delivery, split payouts to restaurants and drivers, refund processing, ledger management.","boundaries":"Owns PaymentTransaction and Ledger aggregates. Integrates with external payment processor (Stripe/Adyen). Publishes PaymentAuthorized, PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued events."},{"name":"Identity & Auth Service","responsibility":"User registration, authentication (JWT/OAuth), role management (customer, driver, restaurant_admin, platform_admin), profile management.","boundaries":"Owns User/Account aggregate. Generic subdomain — use off-the-shelf where possible (Auth0, Cognito). Issues tokens consumed by all gateways."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Sends push notifications, SMS, and emails triggered by domain events (order confirmed, driver arriving, delivery complete, promotions).","boundaries":"Owns no business state. Subscribes to events from Order, Delivery, and Payment contexts. Manages delivery channel preferences and templates."},{"name":"Rating & Review Service","responsibility":"Collects and serves ratings for restaurants, menu items, and drivers. Calculates aggregate scores.","boundaries":"Owns Rating aggregate. Triggered by OrderCompleted event. Feeds back into Restaurant Catalog search ranking."},{"name":"Event Bus","responsibility":"Asynchronous message backbone. Enables loose coupling between all contexts via publish/subscribe.","boundaries":"Infrastructure component. No business logic. Guarantees at-least-once delivery. Candidates: Kafka, AWS SNS+SQS, or NATS."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Customer Gateway","to":"Restaurant Catalog Service","contract":"GET /restaurants?lat,lng,cuisine — returns paginated restaurant list with menus","coupling_type":"synchronous query"},{"from":"Customer Gateway","to":"Order Management Service","contract":"POST /orders {restaurantId, items[], deliveryAddress, paymentMethodId} — returns orderId + status","coupling_type":"synchronous command"},{"from":"Customer Gateway","to":"Delivery Service","contract":"GET /orders/{id}/tracking — returns driver location, ETA (WebSocket for real-time)","coupling_type":"synchronous query + WebSocket"},{"from":"Order Management Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"AuthorizePayment command via event bus; PaymentAuthorized/PaymentFailed response event","coupling_type":"asynchronous command/event"},{"from":"Order Management Service","to":"Delivery Service","contract":"OrderConfirmed event triggers dispatch; DriverAssigned event flows back","coupling_type":"asynchronous event"},{"from":"Order Management Service","to":"Restaurant Catalog Service","contract":"ValidateMenu query (sync) on order placement to verify item availability and pricing","coupling_type":"synchronous query"},{"from":"Restaurant Gateway","to":"Order Management Service","contract":"POST /orders/{id}/accept, POST /orders/{id}/ready — restaurant confirms and signals readiness","coupling_type":"synchronous command"},{"from":"Delivery Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"DriverAssigned, DriverArriving, DeliveryCompleted events trigger customer/restaurant notifications","coupling_type":"asynchronous event"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued events trigger receipt/refund notifications","coupling_type":"asynchronous event"},{"from":"Rating & Review Service","to":"Restaurant Catalog Service","contract":"AggregateRatingUpdated event updates search ranking scores","coupling_type":"asynchronous event"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer browses restaurants (sync read from Catalog). 2. Customer places order → Order Service validates items (sync call to Catalog), authorizes payment (async command to Payment), and persists Order in Created state. 3. On PaymentAuthorized, Order transitions to Confirmed and publishes OrderConfirmed. 4. Restaurant receives notification, accepts, marks preparing/ready. 5. Delivery Service receives OrderConfirmed, runs dispatch algorithm, assigns driver, publishes DriverAssigned. 6. Driver picks up → PickedUp event. Driver delivers → DeliveryCompleted event. 7. Order Service transitions to Completed, triggers PaymentCapture (async to Payment). 8. Notification Service reacts to key events throughout, pushing updates to all three parties. 9. After completion, Rating Service prompts customer for review.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Saga / Process Manager","rationale":"The order lifecycle spans multiple services (payment auth → restaurant confirm → driver dispatch → delivery → payment capture). A saga in the Order Service orchestrates this, handling compensating actions (refunds, cancellations) on failure at any step."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture","rationale":"Loose coupling between bounded contexts. Each context publishes domain events; others subscribe. Enables independent scaling and deployment. Critical for real-time updates to three different user types."},{"pattern":"Backend-for-Frontend (BFF)","rationale":"Three distinct client types (customer app, restaurant dashboard, driver app) have very different data needs. Dedicated gateways avoid a one-size-fits-all API that couples all clients."},{"pattern":"CQRS (within Order and Catalog)","rationale":"Order writes go through the state machine with full validation; reads (order history, tracking) are served from denormalized projections. Catalog writes (menu updates) are low-frequency; reads (search/browse) are high-frequency and served from a search index."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig (deployment strategy)","rationale":"If migrating from a monolith, extract bounded contexts one at a time starting with the core (Order Management), using an anti-corruption layer to bridge old and new."},{"pattern":"Outbox Pattern","rationale":"Ensures atomicity between persisting state changes and publishing events. Prevents lost events when a service commits to its DB but crashes before publishing."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, items, status, customerId, restaurantId, deliveryId, paymentId — invariants: valid state transitions, item totals match payment amount, cancellation rules)","OrderItem (value object: menuItemId, name, quantity, unitPrice, customizations)"]},{"name":"Delivery & Logistics","classification":"core","aggregates":["Driver (root: driverId, status [available/busy/offline], currentLocation, activeDeliveryId — invariants: one active delivery at a time, must be available to accept)","Delivery (root: deliveryId, orderId, driverId, pickupLocation, dropoffLocation, status, estimatedArrival — invariants: valid status transitions)"]},{"name":"Restaurant Catalog","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Restaurant (root: restaurantId, name, address, serviceArea, hours, isActive — invariants: menu items must have positive prices, service area must be valid polygon)","Menu (root: menuId, restaurantId, categories[], items[] — invariants: at least one active item when restaurant is active)","MenuItem (entity within Menu: itemId, name, price, available, customizationOptions[])"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"core","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: transactionId, orderId, amount, status [authorized/captured/refunded/failed], paymentMethodId — invariants: capture ≤ authorized amount, refund ≤ captured amount)","Payout (root: payoutId, recipientId, recipientType [restaurant/driver], amount, period, status)"]},{"name":"Identity","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Account (root: accountId, email, role, authProvider, profile)"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationPreference (root: userId, channels[], templates[] — no complex invariants)"]},{"name":"Rating","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Review (root: reviewId, orderId, targetId, targetType [restaurant/driver], score, comment, createdAt — invariants: one review per order per target type, score 1-5)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Delivery & Logistics","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order Management publishes OrderConfirmed; Delivery subscribes and initiates dispatch. Delivery publishes status events back."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order Management commands payment authorization and capture. Payment responds with success/failure events."},{"upstream":"Restaurant Catalog","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Conformist. Order Management conforms to Catalog's menu model for item validation. Uses an ACL to translate menu items into order line items."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Open Host Service. Order Management publishes standardized events; Notification subscribes and formats for delivery channels."},{"upstream":"Delivery & Logistics","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"Open Host Service. Delivery publishes tracking events; Notification formats and sends to customers."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Rating","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. OrderCompleted event triggers review solicitation."},{"upstream":"Identity","downstream":"All Contexts","relationship":"Generic Subdomain / Shared Kernel. JWT tokens issued by Identity are consumed by all services for authN/authZ. Minimal shared kernel — only userId and role claims."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Event-driven async communication between contexts instead of synchronous REST calls","gains":"Loose coupling, independent deployability and scalability, resilience to individual service failures, natural audit trail","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency (customer might briefly see stale status), increased infrastructure complexity (event bus, dead-letter queues), harder to debug distributed flows","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous REST orchestration — simpler to reason about but creates tight coupling and cascading failures","Hybrid: sync for the critical path (order placement), async for the rest — viable as a stepping stone"]},{"decision":"Saga pattern in Order Management rather than distributed transactions","gains":"No two-phase commit needed, each service owns its data, compensating actions are explicit and testable","sacrifices":"Saga logic is complex, must handle every failure mode with compensating actions, eventual consistency during in-flight sagas","alternatives_considered":["Two-phase commit — strong consistency but doesn't scale, couples all participants","Choreography-based saga — no central orchestrator but harder to reason about and monitor; chose orchestration for visibility"]},{"decision":"Three separate BFF gateways instead of a unified API gateway","gains":"Each client type gets a tailored API, independent evolution, no bloated god-gateway","sacrifices":"More services to deploy and maintain, some duplication of auth middleware","alternatives_considered":["Single API gateway with client-specific routes — simpler ops but tends toward a monolithic gateway over time","GraphQL federation — flexible but adds complexity for mobile clients and real-time (WebSocket) use cases"]},{"decision":"Order Management as orchestrating core domain","gains":"Single source of truth for order state, clear ownership of the most critical business process, centralized saga management","sacrifices":"Order service becomes a coordination bottleneck — must be highly available and carefully scaled","alternatives_considered":["Choreography with no central orchestrator — more resilient but much harder to monitor and debug order flows"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Dispatch Algorithm","evolution_path":"Start with simple nearest-available-driver matching. Evolve to ML-based optimization considering driver capacity, traffic, batching multiple orders, and predicted preparation time. Algorithm is encapsulated behind a DispatchStrategy interface inside the Delivery context."},{"area":"Payment Provider","evolution_path":"Payment Service wraps the external provider behind a PaymentGateway port. Swap Stripe for Adyen or add crypto payments by implementing a new adapter — no changes to Order or other contexts."},{"area":"Search & Discovery","evolution_path":"Start with basic geo+cuisine filtering on Postgres. Evolve to Elasticsearch/Algolia for full-text search, personalized recommendations, and sponsored listings. The Catalog read path is already separated via CQRS."},{"area":"Notification Channels","evolution_path":"Add new channels (WhatsApp, in-app, voice) by adding adapters in the Notification Service. Event contracts from upstream contexts remain unchanged."},{"area":"Pricing & Promotions","evolution_path":"Currently embedded in Order validation. Can extract into a dedicated Pricing context when promo logic becomes complex (surge pricing, loyalty discounts, restaurant deals). The seam is the price calculation step in order placement."},{"area":"Multi-Tenant / Multi-Region","evolution_path":"Each context is independently deployable. Add region-based routing at the gateway layer. Data residency handled per-context based on regulatory needs."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order State Machine","reason":"The order lifecycle (Created→Confirmed→Preparing→ReadyForPickup→PickedUp→Delivered→Completed) is the backbone of the platform. All three user types and all downstream contexts depend on these states and transitions. Changing them is a coordinated, high-risk effort — keep this rigid and well-tested."},{"area":"Payment Authorization → Capture Flow","reason":"Financial correctness is non-negotiable. The two-phase auth-then-capture flow with compensating refunds must be rock-solid. Changes here require careful audit and compliance review."},{"area":"Event Contracts","reason":"Published domain events are the integration contracts between contexts. Breaking changes require versioned events and consumer migration. Treat event schemas as public APIs — additive changes only, no field removals without deprecation."},{"area":"Driver Assignment Invariant","reason":"One driver, one active delivery at a time (until batching is introduced). This invariant prevents double-booking and is enforced at the aggregate level. Relaxing it for order batching is a deliberate, planned evolution — not a casual change."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Order Service as single point of failure — all order flow passes through it","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy with high availability (multi-AZ, auto-scaling). Implement circuit breakers on downstream calls. Use the outbox pattern so no events are lost on crash. Consider active-passive failover for the saga orchestrator."},{"risk":"Real-time driver location tracking at scale creates high write throughput","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use a specialized store for location data (Redis with geo commands, or a time-series DB). Don't route location updates through the main Delivery Service DB. Batch location writes and serve reads from a cache with 2-3 second staleness tolerance."},{"risk":"Eventual consistency confuses users — e.g., order shows 'placed' but payment hasn't confirmed yet","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use optimistic UI with clear status indicators ('confirming payment...'). Design the saga to complete the critical path (place → auth → confirm) within 2-3 seconds. If it takes longer, show explicit pending state rather than stale data."},{"risk":"Dispatch algorithm cold-start — no drivers available in a zone","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement fallback: expand search radius progressively, queue orders with TTL, notify customer of delay. Add surge incentives to attract drivers to underserved zones."},{"risk":"Restaurant doesn't accept order in time","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Auto-cancel with refund after configurable timeout (e.g., 5 minutes). Notify customer immediately. Track restaurant acceptance rates and adjust ranking/visibility for unreliable restaurants."},{"risk":"Event bus failure causes order flow to stall","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Use a durable, replicated event bus (Kafka with replication factor 3). Implement the outbox pattern so events are never lost. Add dead-letter queues with alerting. Design compensating timeouts in the saga — if no response event within N seconds, retry or compensate."},{"risk":"Split payment calculation errors (platform fee + restaurant payout + driver payout ≠ customer charge)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Enforce the invariant that splits are calculated atomically within the Payment context using a ledger with double-entry bookkeeping. All money movements must balance to zero. Automated reconciliation job runs hourly."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Start with a modular monolith organized by bounded context. Each context is a separate module with its own domain model, persistence, and public event interface — but deployed as a single unit initially. Extract into independent services as scale demands, starting with Delivery (real-time location is the first scaling bottleneck) and Payment (compliance isolation). Use a local event bus (in-process) that can be swapped for Kafka when you split services. This gives you the architectural benefits of bounded contexts without the operational overhead of microservices on day one.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/order/ — Order aggregate, OrderSaga, OrderRepository, OrderEvents","src/delivery/ — Driver aggregate, Delivery aggregate, DispatchStrategy, LocationTracker","src/catalog/ — Restaurant aggregate, Menu aggregate, SearchIndex projection","src/payment/ — PaymentTransaction aggregate, Ledger, PaymentGatewayPort + StripeAdapter","src/identity/ — Account aggregate, AuthService, JWTMiddleware","src/notification/ — NotificationService, ChannelAdapters (push, sms, email), EventSubscribers","src/rating/ — Review aggregate, AggregateScoreProjection","src/shared/events/ — Event base types, EventBus interface, OutboxTable","src/gateway/customer/ — Customer BFF routes","src/gateway/restaurant/ — Restaurant BFF routes","src/gateway/driver/ — Driver BFF routes","infra/ — Docker compose, event bus config, database migrations per context"],"patterns_to_follow":"Hexagonal architecture within each context (ports & adapters). Domain model at the center, application services orchestrating use cases, infrastructure adapters at the edges. Each context exposes only its public API and published events — no direct DB access across contexts. Use the repository pattern for persistence, strategy pattern for swappable algorithms (dispatch, pricing), and the outbox pattern for reliable event publishing.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Sharing a database between contexts — this creates hidden coupling and defeats the purpose of bounded contexts","Synchronous chains longer than 2 hops — if A calls B calls C calls D, you have a fragile distributed monolith","Putting business logic in the gateway layer — gateways should only aggregate and transform, not decide","Over-engineering the dispatch algorithm before you have driver data — start simple, iterate with real data","Skipping the outbox pattern — publishing events outside the DB transaction WILL lose events under failure","Building all microservices from day one — start modular monolith, extract when you have evidence of where the scaling pain actually is","Neglecting idempotency — with at-least-once event delivery, every handler must be idempotent or you'll get duplicate charges and deliveries"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Bounded context decomposition — these are well-established domain boundaries for food delivery","Order saga pattern — this is the standard approach for multi-step order flows","Event-driven integration between contexts — proven at scale by Uber Eats, DoorDash, etc.","BFF gateway pattern for three distinct client types"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact dispatch algorithm design — depends heavily on driver density, geography, and order volume data that doesn't exist yet","Pricing/promotions complexity — may warrant its own bounded context sooner than suggested, depending on business strategy","Read model technology choices (Elasticsearch vs Postgres full-text vs Algolia) — depends on search requirements and budget"],"assumptions":["Platform operates in a single country/currency initially — multi-currency adds significant Payment complexity","Order volume is moderate at launch (< 10K orders/day) — justifies starting with modular monolith","Restaurants manage their own menus — no centralized menu data entry team","Drivers are independent contractors, not employees — affects payout and scheduling model","Mobile apps are the primary client — influences BFF design and real-time requirements"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"A food delivery platform connecting restaurants, customers, and delivery drivers — designed as a modular system with six bounded contexts. The architecture uses event-driven communication between contexts to keep coupling low, a central Order context as the orchestrating core domain, and clear separation between the real-time operational path (ordering, dispatch, tracking) and the analytical/back-office path. The design prioritizes independent deployability of each context,
